10.text: Standard library math, used later for log in the bigram log-likelihood calculation.

11.text: Filesystem ops, building a portable path to the shared dataset.

14.text: Pin the random seed so every run sees the same training order.

19.text: Portable path that works regardless of where you run the script from.

20.text: Auto-download the dataset on first run.

21.text: Lazy import, only needed if the file is missing.

23.text: Karpathy's names dataset: ~32K human names, one per line.

24.text: Fetch the file from the internet once and save it locally. After this first download, every future run loads instantly from disk.

26.text: Load every name, strip whitespace, skip blanks. Each name becomes one "document" for training.

27.text: Shuffle so the model doesn't memorize alphabetical order.

28.text: A quick sanity check that we loaded the full dataset. With ~32K names, we have plenty of data to discover meaningful merge patterns.

34.text: Collect every unique character (a-z), this becomes the base vocabulary, same as lab 01.

35.text: Character-to-integer mapping. In labs 01-08, this WAS the tokenizer. Now it's the baseline we'll beat.

36.text: Reverse mapping for decoding back to text.

37.text: BOS (beginning/end of sequence) gets the next available id, just past the last character.

38.text: 27 tokens total: 26 letters + BOS. Tiny vocab, but each token is just one character.

46.text: Wrap each name with BOS markers on both ends, the model learns to start and stop generating.

48.text: BOS + character ids + BOS. Identical to how labs 01-08 encode, just factored into a function.

51.text: Reverse the encoding, filtering out BOS markers to recover the original name.

53.text: Skip BOS tokens, map ids back to characters, join into a string.

71.text: 50 merges is tiny, GPT-2 uses 50,000. But even 50 will noticeably compress our names corpus.

76.text: Reuse the same BOS id so char and BPE tokenizers are comparable.

77.text: 27 base tokens before any merges happen.

81.text: The corpus starts as character-level token sequences, identical to what the char tokenizer produces.

83.text: Wrap with BOS, convert characters to integer ids. This is the starting point BPE will compress.

84.text: Accumulate each tokenized name into the corpus list. This gives us the complete training set that BPE will iteratively compress through merges.

87.text: The heart of BPE: count how often each adjacent pair of tokens appears across the entire corpus.

89.text: Simple dictionary to accumulate pair frequencies.

90.text: Scan every tokenized name in the corpus.

91.text: Slide a window of size 2 across each sequence.

92.text: Each pair is a tuple of two adjacent token ids.

93.text: Increment the count. The pair with the highest count will be merged next.

97.text: Once we find the winning pair, replace every occurrence with a brand new token id.

99.text: Allocate a fresh corpus list that will hold the post-merge versions of every sequence. We rebuild rather than mutate in place to keep the logic clean.

101.text: Walk through the old sequence building a new one token by token, replacing any occurrence of the target pair with the merged token along the way.

102.text: Manual index, we sometimes skip ahead by 2 when we find the pair.

103.text: Walk through the sequence looking for the target pair.

104.text: Found the pair! Both tokens match at adjacent positions.

105.text: Replace the pair with the single merged token, this is where compression happens.

106.text: Skip past both original tokens since we consumed them as one.

108.text: No match here, keep the original token.

109.text: Move the scan pointer forward by one position. We only skip by two when we find and merge the target pair; otherwise we advance normally.

110.text: This name is done, add its merged sequence to the new corpus.

117.text: The merge table: maps each learned pair to its new token id. This IS the tokenizer.

118.text: Tracks the growing vocabulary size as merges create new tokens.

121.text: Recursively decode a BPE token back to its string. Merged tokens unpack into their components.

123.text: BOS decodes to empty string, it's a control token, not a character.

125.text: Base characters decode directly via the character mapping.

128.text: For merged tokens, find which pair was merged and recursively decode both halves.

129.text: Linear scan through merges, fine for 50 merges, would need a reverse map at scale.

130.text: Recursive call: a merged token might itself be composed of merged tokens.

134.text: Human-friendly token display for the training log.

136.text: Show BOS as a readable tag.

140.text: Merged tokens show their full decoded string (e.g., token 28 might display as "an").

148.text: The main BPE training loop: 50 rounds of find-best-pair, merge, repeat.

149.text: Count all adjacent pairs in the current corpus.

150.text: Safety check, if every sequence is length 1, nothing left to merge.

154.text: Greedily pick the most frequent pair. This greedy choice is what makes BPE BPE.

155.text: Track the frequency for the training log.

156.text: New token id = current vocab size (incrementing from 27).

157.text: Record this merge in the table. During encoding, merges are replayed in this exact order.

159.text: Log the first 20 merges and every 10th after, watch common English patterns emerge ("an", "er", "in").

160.text: Show what character sequences are being fused together.

163.text: Apply the merge to the entire corpus, every occurrence of the pair becomes one token.

164.text: Vocabulary grows by one with each merge.

169.text: Decode a full sequence of BPE token ids back to a readable string.

171.text: Concatenate the decoded string for each token in the sequence.

177.text: Encoding with BPE: start from characters, then replay all merges in order. This is how GPT-2 encodes text.

180.text: Start exactly like character encoding, BOS + char ids + BOS.

182.text: Replay merges in training order. Earlier merges are higher priority (more frequent patterns).

183.text: Apply each merge to the token sequence. After all merges, common subwords are single tokens.

193.text: A mix of short, long, common, and unusual names to show compression varies.

195.text: Add some random names so the examples aren't cherry-picked.

197.text: Remove duplicates while preserving order, cap at 10.

201.text: For each example, encode with both tokenizers and show the compression ratio.

202.text: Character encoding: always len(name) + 2 tokens (one per char plus two BOS markers).

203.text: BPE encoding: fewer tokens because common patterns like "an", "er" are now single tokens.

204.text: Compression ratio, how many character tokens each BPE token replaces on average.

205.text: Show the BPE tokenization visually, so you can see subword boundaries.

216.text: Sum up character tokens across the entire corpus.

217.text: Sum up BPE tokens across the entire corpus, this is the real compression measurement.

218.text: Average tokens per name with character encoding.

219.text: Average tokens per name with BPE, expect roughly 1.3-1.5x shorter.

228.text: The headline number: BPE compresses the corpus by ~1.3-1.5x with just 50 merges.

241.text: Display the full merge table, this IS the BPE vocabulary, showing exactly what patterns the algorithm discovered.

242.text: Decode each merged token to see its string representation.

243.text: Show the components that were merged (which might themselves be merged tokens).

262.text: A bigram model is the simplest possible language model: just a transition probability table. No neural network needed.

265.text: 2D count matrix: counts[a][b] = how often token b follows token a.

268.text: Tally one more occurrence of this token-to-token transition. The row with the highest total count will later define the most probable next tokens.

272.text: Add-1 (Laplace) smoothing prevents zero probabilities for unseen pairs.

273.text: Normalize to a proper probability distribution.

277.text: Generate a name by sampling one token at a time from the bigram distribution.

279.text: Start with BOS, the model will sample what typically follows the start marker.

282.text: Look up the probability distribution for the current (last) token.

283.text: Sample from the cumulative distribution using a uniform random number.

285.text: Walk through tokens by probability until we pass the random threshold.

287.text: This token was selected, the cumulative probability exceeded our random draw.

290.text: Stop generating when we hit BOS again (end of name).

295.text: Average log-likelihood per token measures how well the model predicts the training data.

297.text: Accumulate total log-likelihood across all tokens.

300.text: For each adjacent pair in each sequence, look up its probability.

301.text: The probability assigned to this actual transition, higher is better.

303.text: Log probability, the fundamental unit of information theory. Sums are easier than products.

305.text: Average over all tokens gives a per-token measure, comparable across different vocab sizes.

310.text: Encode the entire corpus with the character tokenizer for bigram training.

311.text: Train the character-level bigram, just counting transitions in a 27x27 table.

312.text: Measure how well it models the data. Lower perplexity = better model.

319.text: Encode the entire corpus with BPE, shorter sequences but larger vocabulary.

320.text: Train the BPE bigram, a 77x77 transition table. Each step now covers more text.

321.text: The BPE bigram should have better perplexity per token because each token carries more information.

334.text: Generate from the character bigram, expect babble-ish names since bigrams have no long-range memory.

335.text: Sample a complete name by repeatedly drawing from the transition table.

336.text: Decode character ids back to a readable string.

339.text: Generate from the BPE bigram, each step jumps through common letter patterns, so names sound a bit more natural.

341.text: Sample from the BPE bigram's 77x77 transition table.

342.text: Decode BPE tokens back to a string, merged tokens expand to multiple characters.
